title: Making Plans with a Friend
situation: Inviting a friend out and settling a time and place together.
skills: using gaan + infinitive for near-future plans

phrases.hindi: Heb je zin om koffie te drinken?
phrases.english: Do you feel like getting a coffee?
phrases.context: 'Zin hebben om ... te + infinitive' (the base or dictionary form of the verb) is the standard Dutch way to ask if someone wants to do something. More direct than 'wil je' and warmer in tone. Use it for casual invitations between friends.
phrases.pronunciation: hep ye ZIN om KOF-fee te DRINK-en?

phrases.hindi: Zullen we naar de film gaan?
phrases.english: Shall we go to the cinema?
phrases.context: 'Zullen we ...?' is the go-to phrase for suggesting an activity together. It is softer than a direct statement and leaves room for the other person to adjust the plan. Very common between friends and colleagues.
phrases.pronunciation: ZUL-len we naar de FILM chaan?

phrases.hindi: Wat ga je dit weekend doen?
phrases.english: What are you going to do this weekend?
phrases.context: Uses 'gaan + infinitive' (the base or dictionary form of the verb) to ask about near-future plans. A natural conversation opener before making an invitation. The verb 'gaan' conjugates and the infinitive goes to the end of the sentence.
phrases.pronunciation: vat CHAA ye dit VAYK-ent DOON?

phrases.english: When does it suit you? / When works for you?
phrases.context: 'Uitkomen' is a separable verb meaning to suit or be convenient. Here it splits: 'komt' stays near the front and 'uit' moves to the end. Note 'jou' (stressed object form) rather than unstressed 'je', which adds slight emphasis to the 'you'.

'Passen' here means to suit or fit a schedule, not to try on clothing. This is the short, friendly version of 'Komt zaterdag je uit?'. Very common in informal planning. You can swap in any day: 'Past vrijdag je?'

'Dat is goed' is a calm, clear acceptance. 'Leuk!' adds warmth and enthusiasm. Both are natural responses when a suggested time or activity fits. You can combine them: 'Dat is goed, leuk!' for a genuinely positive reply.

phrases.hindi: Ik kan helaas niet, ik heb al iets.
phrases.english: Unfortunately I can't, I already have something on.
phrases.context: 'Helaas' softens the decline politely. 'Al iets hebben' (I already have something) is the standard Dutch way to say you are busy without going into detail. Follow it with an alternative if you want to keep the plan alive: 'Kan het ook op zondag?'
phrases.pronunciation: ik kan he-LAAS NEET, ik hep al EETS.

phrases.context: 'Afspreken' is a separable verb meaning to make an appointment or agree to meet. It splits here: 'spreken' at the front, 'af' at the end. 'Hoe laat' asks for a specific clock time. This is usually one of the last questions before a plan is settled.

phrases.hindi: Zullen we om acht uur afspreken?
phrases.english: Shall we meet at eight o'clock?
phrases.context: Combining 'Zullen we ...?' for the suggestion with 'afspreken'. Here 'afspreken' is the infinitive (the base or dictionary form), so the prefix 'af' stays attached and the whole verb goes to the end. It only splits when it is the finite verb, as in 'Hoe laat spreken we af?'. 'Om' before the time is required in Dutch: always 'om acht uur', not 'at acht uur'.
phrases.pronunciation: ZUL-len we om ACHT uur AF-spray-ken?

phrases.context: 'Tot' + day is the natural Dutch way to sign off once a plan is confirmed. It is warm and concrete. You can use it in chat, on the phone, or in person: 'Tot morgen', 'Tot vrijdag', 'Tot dan!'

grammar_notes: 'Zin hebben om ... te + infinitive' (the base or dictionary form of the verb): this pattern expresses wanting or feeling like doing something. The verb after 'te' is always the infinitive: 'Heb je zin om te wandelen?', 'Heb je zin om een film te zien?'. Drop 'om' and you get a slightly more abrupt version.

'Zullen we ...?' for suggestions: 'zullen' is a modal verb. In the 'zullen we ...?' pattern it works as a soft suggestion or invitation, equivalent to 'shall we ...?'. The main verb follows as an infinitive (the base or dictionary form) at the end: 'Zullen we lunchen?', 'Zullen we morgen bellen?'

Near future with 'gaan + infinitive': Dutch uses 'gaan' + infinitive (the base or dictionary form of the verb) for concrete near-future plans, the same way English uses 'going to'. 'Gaan' conjugates normally: ik ga, jij gaat, hij gaat, we gaan. The infinitive always goes to the end: 'Ik ga vanavond koken.'

'Afspreken' as a separable verb: in Dutch, many verbs have a prefix that detaches and moves to the end of the clause. 'Afspreken' (to arrange to meet) splits into 'spreken ... af': 'Hoe laat spreken we af?' In the infinitive (the base or dictionary form) it stays together: 'om af te spreken'. The same pattern applies to 'uitkomen' (to be convenient): 'Komt het jou uit?' / 'om uit te komen'.

'Je' vs 'jou': 'je' is the unstressed object form used in everyday speech. 'Jou' is the stressed object form used when you want to put weight on the 'you': 'Past zaterdag je?' (neutral) vs 'Komt het jou uit?' (slight emphasis on the person being asked). After a preposition you also use the object form, and the stressed 'jou' is the more common choice there: 'voor jou', 'met jou'.
culture_notes: Dutch people tend to plan ahead. Calling a friend on Friday to meet that same evening can feel presumptuous. A week or two in advance is the norm for social plans, and checking someone's 'agenda' (diary) before suggesting a date is considered considerate, not overly formal.

Splitting costs is the default among Dutch friends, even close ones. 'Ieder betaalt voor zichzelf' (everyone pays for themselves) is assumed unless someone explicitly says 'ik trakteer' (my treat). Do not wait for the other person to offer to split.

Punctuality matters. Arriving five to ten minutes late to a social plan is normal, but more than fifteen minutes warrants a quick message. Being on time is a form of respect and the Dutch notice when it is missing.

Concrete plans beat vague intentions. 'We moeten een keer koffiedrinken' (we should grab a coffee sometime) is a common polite phrase, but Dutch friends expect you to follow through with a real date. If you say it, send a message with a suggestion within a day or two.

skill_breakdown.explanation: The two key patterns are 'Zullen we ...?' (shall we ...?) and 'Heb je zin om ... te + infinitive?' (do you feel like ...?). 'Zullen we ...?' is slightly more spontaneous; 'Heb je zin om ...?' gives the other person more space to say no. Both are very common in everyday Dutch conversation.

more_examples.english: Shall we go for a walk this weekend?

more_examples.hindi: Heb je zin om vanavond te eten?
more_examples.english: Do you feel like having dinner tonight?
Shall we sit on a terrace? (very common in summer)

more_examples.hindi: Heb je zin om samen te fietsen?
more_examples.english: Do you feel like going for a bike ride together?

skill_breakdown.explanation: Accepting is simple: 'Dat is goed', 'Leuk!', or 'Prima, afgesproken!' Declining politely uses 'helaas' (unfortunately) plus a reason, then often a counter-offer. Offering an alternative shows you still want to meet, just not at that time.

more_examples.english: Great, it's a deal! / Perfect, agreed!

more_examples.hindi: Ik kan zaterdag niet, maar zondag kan ik wel.
more_examples.english: I can't on Saturday, but I can on Sunday.

more_examples.hindi: Helaas, ik werk die dag. Een andere keer?
more_examples.english: Unfortunately I'm working that day. Another time?
That sounds lovely! / I'd enjoy that!

practice_prompt: You are Femke, a close Dutch friend of the user. You are texting to make plans for the coming weekend. Use informal 'je/jij' throughout as close friends do. Open verbatim with: 'Hé! Heb je dit weekend al iets?' Then invite them to join you for a film on Saturday evening at Pathé, suggest meeting at eight in the city centre, and find out whether Saturday works. If they decline, offer Sunday afternoon for a coffee instead and settle on a concrete time and place before signing off. Weave in 'Zullen we ...?', 'Heb je zin om ...?', 'Past zaterdag je?', 'Hoe laat spreken we af?', and close with 'Tot dan!' once the plan is confirmed. Keep the register warm and direct, the way close Dutch friends text. Flag mistakes with [[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]], particularly: missing 'te' in 'zin om ... te + infinitive', wrong position of the separable prefix 'af' in 'afspreken', 'gaan' used without an infinitive at the end, and 'je' vs 'jou' stress errors. Stay in Dutch throughout. Aim for a natural 6-8 turn arc ending with a settled plan and a friendly 'Tot zaterdag!' or 'Tot zondag!'
